content:
# Exodus Chapter 9 -- Disagreement Log

**Arbiter:** SOPHIA | **Filed:** 2026-03-04

---

## 1. v.3 -- "A very heavy pestilence" vs. "a devastating plague" (*dever kaved me'od*)

| Translator | Rendering | Approach |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "a dever [pestilence] very heavy [kaved]" | Literal: Hebrew word order, transliterated with gloss |
| LOGOS | "a very heavy pestilence" | Formal: English adjective order, preserves *kaved* |
| PNEUMA | "a devastating plague" | Dynamic: interpretive, loses *kaved* wordplay |

**Hebrew:** דֶּבֶר כָּבֵד מְאֹד (*dever kaved me'od*) -- "pestilence heavy very." The adjective *kaved* is the same root used for heart-hardening throughout the chapter and the entire plague narrative.

**LXX:** θάνατος μέγας σφόδρα (*thanatos megas sphodra*) -- "a very great death."

**Nature of disagreement:** The critical issue is whether to preserve the *kaved* wordplay. EMET and LOGOS both retain "heavy" (EMET in Hebrew order, LOGOS in English order), keeping the connection between the plague's severity and Pharaoh's heart-heaviness. PNEUMA substitutes "devastating," which communicates the meaning but severs the wordplay. In a chapter where *kaved* appears six times across plague descriptions and heart-hardening, this connection is not incidental but architecturally essential.

**SOPHIA's resolution:** "a very heavy pestilence" -- following LOGOS. English adjective order for readability; "heavy" preserved for the *kaved* wordplay.

**Severity:** MODERATE. The meaning is preserved in all three renderings, but the literary architecture of the chapter depends on the *kaved* connection being visible to readers.

---

## 2. v.7 -- *Kaved* qal: "was heavy" vs. "remained heavy" (stative hardening)

| Translator | Rendering | Approach |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "the heart of Par'oh was heavy" | Literal: qal stative rendered directly |
| LOGOS | "the heart of Pharaoh was heavy" | Formal: same as EMET |
| PNEUMA | "Pharaoh's heart remained heavy" | Dynamic: adds durational nuance |

**Hebrew:** וַיִּכְבַּד לֵב פַּרְעֹה (*vayikhbad lev Par'oh*) -- qal of *kaved*, "and was heavy the heart of Pharaoh."

**Nature of disagreement:** PNEUMA adds "remained" -- implying the heaviness was a continuing state from before. While this is a reasonable interpretation (the heart that was heavy in previous chapters is still heavy), the Hebrew verb *vayikhbad* simply describes a state: "was heavy." The durational aspect is PNEUMA's addition.

**SOPHIA's resolution:** "the heart of Pharaoh was heavy" -- following EMET/LOGOS. The straightforward stative rendering. Duration is not encoded in the Hebrew verb form.

**Severity:** LOW. All three convey the same essential meaning. The disagreement concerns whether to import a durational nuance.

---

## 3. v.12 -- *Chazaq* piel: "strengthened" vs. "made unyielding" (THE TURNING POINT)

| Translator | Rendering | Approach |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "YHWH strengthened the heart of Par'oh" | Literal: chazaq = "strengthen," YHWH as agent |
| LOGOS | "the LORD strengthened the heart of Pharaoh" | Formal: same verb, "the LORD" for YHWH |
| PNEUMA | "YHWH made Pharaoh's heart unyielding" | Dynamic: substitutes "made unyielding" for "strengthened" |

**Hebrew:** וַיְחַזֵּק יְהוָה אֶת־לֵב פַּרְעֹה (*vayekhazzeq YHWH et-lev Par'oh*) -- piel of *chazaq* with YHWH as subject, Pharaoh's heart as direct object.

**LXX:** ἐσκλήρυνεν κύριος τὴν καρδίαν Φαραω (*esklērunen kurios tēn kardian Pharaō*) -- "the Lord hardened the heart of Pharaoh."

**Nature of disagreement:** The SOPHIA convention renders *chazaq* consistently as "strengthen." PNEUMA breaks this convention at the most critical moment in the hardening narrative, substituting "made unyielding." While "made unyielding" captures the theological effect, it obscures the verbal continuity between the piel here (YHWH strengthens) and the qal elsewhere (heart "was strengthened"). The whole point of the three-verb system (*chazaq/kaved/qashah*) is that different stems of the same root carry different theological weights. If *chazaq* piel is rendered "made unyielding" while *chazaq* qal is rendered "grew rigid" or "became unyielding" (as PNEUMA does elsewhere), the reader cannot perceive that the same Hebrew root underlies both forms.

**SOPHIA's resolution:** "YHWH strengthened the heart of Pharaoh" -- following EMET/LOGOS. The *chazaq* = "strengthen" convention is maintained. The piel's intensive force is communicated by the fact that YHWH is now the agent, and by the bold/blockquote formatting that marks this as the critical transition.

**Severity:** HIGH. This is the most theologically significant verse in the hardening narrative. The rendering must preserve both the *chazaq* convention (for verbal continuity) and the clarity of divine agency (YHWH as grammatical subject).

---

## 4. v.16 -- *He'emadtikha*: "I have caused you to stand" vs. "I have raised you up"

| Translator | Rendering | Approach |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "I have caused you to stand" | Literal: hiphil of *amad* rendered precisely |
| LOGOS | "I have raised you up" | Formal: follows Pauline/LXX tradition |
| PNEUMA | "I have raised you up" | Dynamic: follows Pauline/LXX tradition |

**Hebrew (MT):** הֶעֱמַדְתִּיךָ (*he'emadtikha*) -- hiphil of *amad* ("to stand"), "I have caused you to stand."

**LXX (Vaticanus):** ἕνεκεν τούτου διετηρήθης (*heneken toutou dietērēthēs*) -- "for this reason you have been preserved."

**Paul (Romans 9:17):** εἰς αὐτὸ τοῦτο ἐξήγειρά σε (*eis auto touto exēgeira se*) -- "for this very purpose I raised you up."

**Nature of disagreement:** This is one of the most discussed text-critical issues in the Pentateuch. Three distinct renderings exist: the MT says God "caused Pharaoh to stand" (sustained him in his position); the LXX says God "preserved" him (kept him alive); Paul's citation says God "raised him up" (brought him onto the stage of history). LOGOS and PNEUMA both render "raised you up," which reflects Paul's Greek (*exegeirō*) rather than the MT's Hebrew (*he'emadtikha*). EMET renders "caused you to stand," preserving the MT's precise verb.

The theological nuance matters: "caused to stand" emphasizes sustaining Pharaoh in his current position; "raised up" implies bringing him into existence or onto the historical stage; "preserved" implies keeping him alive when he could have been destroyed. These are three different claims about divine providence.

**SOPHIA's resolution:** "I have caused you to stand" -- following EMET. The SOPHIA convention follows the MT as the primary text. The distinction between the MT, the LXX, and Paul's citation is a genuine three-way divergence that should not be collapsed. Rendering "raised you up" imports the Pauline/LXX sense into the Hebrew text.

**Severity:** HIGH. This is a text-critical decision with significant theological implications. The rendering determines whether the reader encounters the MT's concept (sustaining in position), the LXX's concept (preserving alive), or Paul's concept (raising onto the stage of history).

---

## 5. v.18 -- "Very heavy hail" vs. "the most devastating hail" (*barad kaved me'od*)

| Translator | Rendering | Approach |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "a very heavy [kaved] hail" | Literal: preserves *kaved* explicitly |
| LOGOS | "a very heavy hail" | Formal: preserves *kaved* |
| PNEUMA | "the most devastating hail" | Dynamic: superlative, loses *kaved* |

**Hebrew:** בָּרָד כָּבֵד מְאֹד (*barad kaved me'od*) -- "hail heavy very."

**Nature of disagreement:** The same issue as v. 3: whether to preserve the *kaved* wordplay. PNEUMA renders "the most devastating," which is superlative rather than the Hebrew's *me'od* ("very") construction. Additionally, "devastating" severs the *kaved* link between plague and heart.

**SOPHIA's resolution:** "a very heavy hail" -- following EMET/LOGOS. The *kaved* wordplay is preserved.

**Severity:** MODERATE. Same reasoning as #1: the literary architecture depends on *kaved* visibility.

---

## 6. v.20 -- "Feared the word of YHWH" vs. "took the word of YHWH seriously"

| Translator | Rendering | Approach |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "feared the word of YHWH" | Literal: *yare* = "feared" |
| LOGOS | "feared the word of the LORD" | Formal: same verb, "the LORD" |
| PNEUMA | "took the word of YHWH seriously" | Dynamic: paraphrases *yare* |

**Hebrew:** הַיָּרֵא אֶת־דְּבַר יְהוָה (*hayare et-devar YHWH*) -- "the one fearing the word of YHWH."

**Nature of disagreement:** PNEUMA dissolves the verb *yare* ("to fear") into the weaker "took seriously." The concept of "fearing YHWH" (*yir'at YHWH*) is a theological cornerstone in Hebrew wisdom and prophetic literature (Proverbs 1:7, 9:10; Psalm 111:10). It should be rendered consistently across the Hebrew Bible. PNEUMA's paraphrase makes the concept more accessible to modern readers but removes the theological-technical term.

**SOPHIA's resolution:** "feared the word of YHWH" -- following EMET/LOGOS. The verb *yare* is preserved. The theological concept of "fearing YHWH" is foundational and should not be paraphrased.

**Severity:** MODERATE. The meaning is similar, but the theological register differs significantly. "Feared" places the Egyptian officials within the biblical category of YHWH-fearers; "took seriously" reduces a theological concept to a psychological description.

---

## 7. v.27 -- *Resha'im*: "wicked ones" vs. "guilty ones" (Pharaoh's confession)

| Translator | Rendering | Approach |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "the wicked ones" | Literal: *resha'im* = "wicked ones" |
| LOGOS | "the wicked ones" | Formal: same |
| PNEUMA | "the guilty ones" | Dynamic: softened legal language |

**Hebrew:** הָרְשָׁעִים (*haresha'im*) -- "the wicked ones." The definite article + plural of *rasha'* ("wicked").

**LXX:** οἱ ἀσεβεῖς (*hoi asebeis*) -- "the ungodly/impious ones."

**Nature of disagreement:** PNEUMA renders *resha'im* as "guilty ones," which captures a legal dimension but misses the moral one. "Guilty" describes a procedural status (found culpable); "wicked" describes a moral character (fundamentally in the wrong). The Hebrew *rasha'* is the antonym of *tsaddiq* ("righteous") -- a fixed pair in wisdom and legal literature. Pharaoh is using the exact antonym pair: YHWH is *tsaddiq*, I am *rasha'*. Rendering the pair as "righteous/guilty" breaks the symmetry; "righteous/wicked" preserves it.

**SOPHIA's resolution:** "the wicked ones" -- following EMET/LOGOS. The *tsaddiq/rasha'* antonym pair is preserved.

**Severity:** MODERATE. The confession's force depends on Pharaoh assigning himself the *rasha'* category -- not just acknowledging procedural guilt but confessing moral wickedness.

---

## 8. v.30 -- "Do not yet fear YHWH God" -- rendering of *mippenei*

| Translator | Rendering | Approach |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "you do not yet fear from before YHWH God" | Literal: preserves *mippenei* ("from before the face of") |
| LOGOS | "you do not yet fear before the LORD God" | Formal: "before" for *mippenei* |
| PNEUMA | "you do not yet truly fear YHWH God" | Dynamic: adds "truly," drops *mippenei* |

**Hebrew:** טֶרֶם תִּירְאוּן מִפְּנֵי יְהוָה אֱלֹהִים (*terem tire'un mippenei YHWH Elohim*) -- "not-yet you-fear from-before YHWH God."

**Nature of disagreement:** The preposition *mippenei* ("from the face of, from before") is part of the idiomatic expression *yare mippenei* ("to fear before"). EMET renders it with full literalness ("from before"), which is wooden. LOGOS compresses to "before," which is clearer. PNEUMA drops *mippenei* entirely and substitutes "truly," which is an interpretation not in the Hebrew. The adverb "truly" implies Pharaoh has a form of fear that is not genuine; the Hebrew merely states he does not yet (*terem*) fear at all.

**SOPHIA's resolution:** "you do not yet fear YHWH God" -- dropping the *mippenei* as an idiom component rather than a semantically distinct element. The verb *yare* ("to fear") absorbs the prepositional force. PNEUMA's "truly" is rejected as an interpretive addition.

**Severity:** LOW. The core meaning (you do not yet fear YHWH) is identical across all three renderings. The disagreement concerns how to handle an idiomatic prepositional phrase.

---

## 9. v.34 -- "He continued to sin" -- all three agree; noted for *khet* significance

| Translator | Rendering | Approach |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "he continued to sin" | Literal |
| LOGOS | "he continued to sin" | Formal |
| PNEUMA | "he sinned again" | Dynamic: simplified |

**Hebrew:** וַיֹּסֶף לַחֲטֹא (*vayosef lakhato*) -- "and he added to sin" (hiphil of *yasaf* + infinitive of *khata'*).

**Nature of disagreement:** Minor. PNEUMA's "sinned again" captures the sense but loses the *yasaf* ("added/continued") nuance. "Continued to sin" implies an ongoing pattern; "sinned again" implies a discrete repetition. The Hebrew *yasaf* + infinitive construct means "to continue doing / to do again," but the former emphasis (continuation of a pattern) fits the narrative context better.

**SOPHIA's resolution:** "he continued to sin" -- following EMET/LOGOS. The *yasaf* construction signals a pattern, not just a single repetition.

**Severity:** LOW. Minimal meaning difference.

---

## 10. v.35 -- *Chazaq* qal: "was strengthened" vs. "became unyielding" (closing formula)

| Translator | Rendering | Approach |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "the heart of Par'oh was strengthened" | Literal: chazaq qal = "was strengthened" |
| LOGOS | "the heart of Pharaoh was strengthened" | Formal: same |
| PNEUMA | "Pharaoh's heart became unyielding" | Dynamic: breaks *chazaq* convention |

**Hebrew:** וַיֶּחֱזַק לֵב פַּרְעֹה (*vayekhezaq lev Par'oh*) -- qal of *chazaq*, "and was strengthened the heart of Pharaoh."

**Nature of disagreement:** Same structural issue as #3: PNEUMA breaks the *chazaq* = "strengthen" convention. "Became unyielding" is a different English verb that cannot be traced back to the same Hebrew root as "strengthened" (9:12), "was strengthened" (7:13, 7:22, 8:19), or the prospective "I will strengthen" (4:21). The convention exists precisely to let readers see the same root across different stems and contexts.

**SOPHIA's resolution:** "the heart of Pharaoh was strengthened" -- following EMET/LOGOS. The *chazaq* convention is maintained.

**Severity:** MODERATE. The convention's integrity across the entire hardening narrative depends on consistent rendering.

---

## 11. Throughout -- PNEUMA's consistent dissolution of *kaved* wordplay

This is not a verse-specific disagreement but a chapter-wide pattern. PNEUMA renders *kaved* differently in nearly every occurrence:

| Verse | Hebrew | EMET/LOGOS | PNEUMA |
|---|---|---|---|
| 9:3 | *dever kaved me'od* | "very heavy pestilence" | "devastating plague" |
| 9:7 | *vayikhbad lev Par'oh* | "heart was heavy" | "heart remained heavy" |
| 9:18 | *barad kaved me'od* | "very heavy hail" | "most devastating hail" |
| 9:24 | *kaved me'od* | "very heavy" | "so severe" |
| 9:34 | *vayakhbed libbo* | "made heavy his heart" | "made his heart heavy" |

The result is that a reader of PNEUMA cannot perceive the literary architecture: the *kaved* root connecting plague-severity to heart-stubbornness. The weight of YHWH's judgment (heavy pestilence, heavy hail) answers the weight of Pharaoh's resistance (heavy heart). This is not an incidental feature but a central literary device of Exodus 9. SOPHIA preserves "heavy" for every *kaved* occurrence.

**Severity:** HIGH (cumulative). No single instance is catastrophic, but the pattern dissolves one of the chapter's most important literary connections.

---

## 12. Recurring convention disagreements

The following disagreements recur from previous chapters and are noted briefly:

**a. Divine name:** LOGOS uses "the LORD"; EMET/PNEUMA use YHWH. SOPHIA follows YHWH per established convention. (Recurring, LOW)

**b. Pronoun capitalization for God:** LOGOS capitalizes divine pronouns (My, He, Me); EMET and PNEUMA do not. SOPHIA follows lowercase convention. (Recurring, LOW)

**c. "Sons of Israel" vs. "Israelites":** EMET uses "sons of Yisra'el"; LOGOS uses "sons of Israel"; PNEUMA uses "Israelites" or "the Israelites." SOPHIA follows "sons of Israel" per convention where the Hebrew reads *benei Yisra'el*. (Recurring, LOW)

**d. Hebrew names:** EMET uses transliterated forms (Mosheh, Par'oh, Aharon, Mitsrayim, Yisra'el); LOGOS and PNEUMA use standard English forms (Moses, Pharaoh, Aaron, Egypt, Israel). SOPHIA follows standard English forms per convention. (Recurring, LOW)

---

*Twelve disagreements logged for Exodus 9 (plus recurring conventions). Two rated HIGH (chazaq piel rendering in v. 12; he'emadtikha text-critical decision in v. 16), one rated HIGH-cumulative (PNEUMA's chapter-wide dissolution of kaved wordplay), four rated MODERATE (dever kaved in v. 3; barad kaved in v. 18; yare rendering in v. 20; resha'im in v. 27; chazaq qal convention in v. 35), and four rated LOW (kaved qal in v. 7; mippenei rendering in v. 30; yasaf in v. 34; recurring conventions). The dominant pattern continues from chapter 8: EMET and LOGOS converge on literal/formal renderings, PNEUMA diverges toward interpretive paraphrase. In this chapter, SOPHIA follows EMET against LOGOS/PNEUMA on the he'emadtikha question (v. 16) -- a rare case where the literal translator's precision is decisive against the formal translator's alignment with the Pauline tradition. SOPHIA follows EMET/LOGOS against PNEUMA on all hardening-verb renderings, maintaining the three-verb convention (chazaq = "strengthen," kaved = "heavy/make heavy," qashah = "make hard") throughout.*

--- SOPHIA
